prompt: Other teams want to react when an order is placed — one to log it, one to bill, and more parties are expected over time, none of which the order code should know about. Which seam mechanism best fits this kind of change?
choices: A strategy interface the order code calls for a single result
An event/hook the order code emits, which interested parties subscribe to
A configuration value resolved per environment
A hard-coded list of consumers inside the order function

prompt: Why can choosing the WRONG seam mechanism be worse than adding no seam at all?
choices: Because any abstraction always improves performance, so the wrong one wastes that gain
Because you still pay the seam's carrying cost, yet the core must be edited each time the change arrives, losing the containment a seam should provide
Because the wrong mechanism is always slower to execute than direct code
Because the compiler rejects mismatched seam mechanisms

prompt: Select ALL signals that suggest a boundary is a genuine axis of change worth a seam now (rather than a speculative one to defer).
choices: You can name two or three concrete variants you would plausibly add
Retrofitting a seam later would ripple across many callers and be expensive
The boundary simply exists between two modules, with no named future variants
Adding the change later would be a cheap, local edit
